title: Pull Requests
question: What is a Pull Request and what is its purpose?
answer: A PR is a proposal to merge changes from one branch to another. It provides a place for code review, discussion, CI results, and approvals before merging.
explanation: PRs are the collaboration primitive of GitHub.
codeExample: gh pr create --fill --base main
bestPractices: Keep PRs small and focused.
commonMistakes: Massive PRs impossible to review.
interviewTips: Discuss draft PRs and review requests.

title: GitHub Actions
question: What are GitHub Actions?
answer: A CI/CD platform built into GitHub. Workflows in .github/workflows/*.yml run on events (push, PR, schedule) using reusable actions.
explanation: Marketplace has thousands of prebuilt actions.
codeExample:
name: ci
on: [push]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
      - run: pnpm i --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm build
bestPractices: Pin action versions by SHA in security-critical workflows.
commonMistakes: Storing secrets in workflow YAML.
interviewTips: Discuss reusable workflows and composite actions.

title: Branch Protection
question: What are branch protection rules?
answer: Rules on protected branches (typically main) requiring PR reviews, passing status checks, signed commits, linear history, etc. before merging.
explanation: Combine with CODEOWNERS for automatic reviewer assignment.
codeExample:
# .github/CODEOWNERS
apps/api/  @backend-team
bestPractices: Require signed commits for high-trust repos.
commonMistakes: Allowing direct pushes to main.
interviewTips: Discuss rulesets (newer, more flexible).

title: Fork vs Clone
question: Difference between fork and clone?
answer: Clone: local copy of a repo. Fork: server-side copy of a repo in your own account, usually to contribute via PRs to the upstream.
explanation: Add the original repo as an upstream remote to keep your fork in sync.
codeExample:
git remote add upstream https://github.com/org/repo.git
git fetch upstream && git rebase upstream/main
bestPractices: Sync forks regularly.
commonMistakes: Losing track of upstream commits.
interviewTips: Explain contributor workflow via forks.

title: Workflow Syntax & Triggers
question: How is a GitHub Actions workflow file structured and how do triggers work?
answer: A workflow lives in .github/workflows/*.yml. Key top-level keys are name, on (events that trigger it), and jobs. The on key can be a single event, a list, or a map with filters like branches, paths, and types.
explanation: Triggers control when a workflow runs. Beyond push and pull_request you can use schedule (cron), workflow_dispatch (manual), and workflow_call (reusable). Path and branch filters avoid unnecessary runs.
codeExample:
name: ci
on:
  push:
    branches: ["main"]
    paths: ["src/**"]
  pull_request:
  workflow_dispatch:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 2 * * 1"
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: echo "triggered by ${{ github.event_name }}"
bestPractices: Use path and branch filters to reduce wasted runs.
Add workflow_dispatch so you can trigger manually.
commonMistakes: Placing workflows outside .github/workflows.
Forgetting that pull_request from forks has restricted permissions.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between pull_request and pull_request_target.

title: Jobs, Steps & Runners
question: What are jobs, steps, and runners in GitHub Actions?
answer: A job is a set of steps that run on the same runner. Steps run sequentially within a job and can be a shell command (run) or an action (uses). Runners are the machines (GitHub-hosted or self-hosted) that execute jobs. Jobs run in parallel by default unless linked with needs.
explanation: Each job gets a fresh runner environment, so state is not shared between jobs unless you use artifacts, outputs, or caching. The needs key creates dependencies and controls order.
codeExample:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo "deploying after tests pass"
bestPractices: Use needs to express real dependencies and enable parallelism elsewhere.
Keep each job focused on one concern.
commonMistakes: Assuming files persist between jobs without artifacts.
Overusing a single giant job that cannot parallelize.
interviewTips: Explain how job outputs pass data downstream.

title: Matrix Builds
question: What is a build matrix and when should you use one?
answer: A matrix runs the same job across multiple combinations of variables (e.g. OS and language versions) in parallel. Define strategy.matrix and reference values with matrix.<key>.
explanation: Matrices give broad coverage cheaply. Use include to add specific combos, exclude to remove them, and fail-fast to control whether one failure cancels the rest.
codeExample:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        node: [18, 20, 22]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node }}
      - run: npm ci && npm test
bestPractices: Set fail-fast: false to see all failures across combinations.
Use include/exclude to trim the matrix to meaningful combos.
commonMistakes: Creating huge matrices that waste CI minutes.
Forgetting to reference the matrix value in runs-on.
interviewTips: Explain how include can add an extra dimension for one combo.

title: Caching Dependencies
question: How do you cache dependencies in GitHub Actions to speed up builds?
answer: Use actions/cache with a key derived from a lockfile hash, or the built-in cache option of setup actions. On a cache hit dependencies are restored; on a miss they are installed and saved for next time.
explanation: Caching avoids re-downloading dependencies each run. A good key changes only when dependencies change (hash of the lockfile), with restore-keys as fallbacks for partial reuse.
codeExample:
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v4
  - uses: actions/cache@v4
    with:
      path: ~/.npm
      key: ${{ runner.os }}-npm-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
      restore-keys: |
        ${{ runner.os }}-npm-
  - run: npm ci
bestPractices: Key caches on a lockfile hash for correctness.
Prefer setup-* actions with built-in caching when available.
commonMistakes: Using a static cache key that never invalidates.
Caching node_modules instead of the package manager cache dir.
interviewTips: Explain restore-keys and partial cache restoration.

title: Artifacts
question: What are workflow artifacts and how do you share files between jobs?
answer: Artifacts are files uploaded from a job (build outputs, test reports, coverage) that can be downloaded by later jobs or after the run. Use actions/upload-artifact and actions/download-artifact.
explanation: Unlike caches (meant for reuse across runs to speed up), artifacts are meant to persist and pass build outputs between jobs or to users. They have retention limits.
codeExample:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
          path: dist/
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: dist
bestPractices: Set sensible retention-days to control storage.
Upload test reports as artifacts for debugging.
commonMistakes: Confusing artifacts with caching.
Uploading huge unnecessary paths.
interviewTips: Contrast artifacts vs cache clearly.

title: Secrets & Environments
question: How do you manage secrets and environments securely in GitHub Actions?
answer: Store sensitive values as repository, organization, or environment secrets and read them via ${{ secrets.NAME }}. Environments add protection rules like required reviewers and wait timers, and can scope secrets to a specific deployment target.
explanation: Secrets are encrypted and masked in logs. Environment-scoped secrets plus deployment protection rules gate production deploys behind approvals.
codeExample:
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://example.com
    steps:
      - run: ./deploy.sh
        env:
          API_KEY: ${{ secrets.PROD_API_KEY }}
bestPractices: Scope secrets to environments and require reviewers for production.
Never echo secrets to logs.
commonMistakes: Hardcoding credentials in YAML.
Exposing secrets to workflows triggered by untrusted forks.
interviewTips: Explain environment protection rules and required reviewers.

title: Reusable Workflows & Composite Actions
question: What is the difference between a reusable workflow and a composite action?
answer: A reusable workflow is a whole workflow called with workflow_call and can define its own jobs, secrets, and inputs. A composite action bundles multiple steps into a single action referenced with uses inside a job. Reusable workflows share whole pipelines; composite actions share step sequences.
explanation: Use reusable workflows to standardize CI across many repos, and composite actions to package a repeated set of steps (setup + build) as one reusable unit.
codeExample:
# caller
jobs:
  ci:
    uses: my-org/.github/.github/workflows/ci.yml@main
    with:
      node-version: 20
    secrets: inherit
# reusable (ci.yml)
on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      node-version:
        type: number
        required: true
bestPractices: Centralize shared workflows in a .github repo.
Version reusable workflows with tags or SHAs.
commonMistakes: Duplicating identical CI across dozens of repos.
Confusing workflow_call inputs with action inputs.
interviewTips: Explain secrets: inherit and input passing.

title: Deployment Environments & CD
question: How do you build a CD pipeline with staged deployment environments?
answer: Model each stage (staging, production) as a GitHub Environment with its own secrets and protection rules. Chain jobs with needs so production only runs after staging succeeds, gated by required reviewers.
explanation: Environments give you approval gates, deployment history, and per-target secrets. Combined with needs and if conditions you get controlled promotion from staging to production.
codeExample:
jobs:
  staging:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - run: ./deploy.sh staging
  production:
    needs: staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - run: ./deploy.sh production
bestPractices: Require manual approval on the production environment.
Promote the exact artifact tested in staging.
commonMistakes: Deploying to prod on every push without gates.
Rebuilding artifacts per environment instead of promoting one.
interviewTips: Discuss blue-green and rollback strategies.

title: CODEOWNERS
question: What is the CODEOWNERS file and how does it work?
answer: CODEOWNERS maps file paths to owners (users or teams) who are automatically requested for review when matching files change. It lives in .github/, root, or docs/ and works with branch protection to require owner approval.
explanation: Later matching patterns take precedence. Combined with required reviews, CODEOWNERS enforces that domain experts approve changes to their areas.
codeExample:
# .github/CODEOWNERS
*           @org/core-maintainers
/apps/api/  @org/backend-team
/apps/web/  @org/frontend-team
*.tf        @org/platform
bestPractices: Use teams rather than individuals for resilience.
Require code owner reviews in branch protection.
commonMistakes: Ordering rules so a broad rule overrides specific ones.
Referencing teams without repo access.
interviewTips: Explain pattern precedence (last match wins).

title: Issue & PR Templates
question: How do issue and pull request templates improve collaboration?
answer: Templates prefill new issues and PRs with a consistent structure. Markdown or YAML issue forms live in .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/, and PR templates in .github/pull_request_template.md, prompting authors for reproduction steps, context, and checklists.
explanation: Structured templates reduce back-and-forth by capturing required information upfront and can route issues via labels and assignees.
codeExample:
# .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug.yml
name: Bug Report
description: Report a bug
labels: [bug]
body:
  - type: textarea
    id: steps
    attributes:
      label: Steps to reproduce
    validations:
      required: true
bestPractices: Use YAML issue forms with required fields.
Add a PR checklist for tests and docs.
commonMistakes: Overly long templates that discourage reporting.
Not marking key fields as required.
interviewTips: Mention config.yml to disable blank issues.

title: GITHUB_TOKEN & Permissions
question: What is GITHUB_TOKEN and how do you scope its permissions?
answer: GITHUB_TOKEN is an automatically created, short-lived token available to each workflow run for authenticating to the GitHub API and the repo. Use the permissions key to grant least-privilege scopes (e.g. contents: read) at the workflow or job level.
explanation: By default the token may have broad permissions; setting explicit permissions follows least privilege. The token is scoped to the repository and expires when the job ends.
codeExample:
permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
jobs:
  label:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: gh pr edit ${{ github.event.number }} --add-label triaged
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
bestPractices: Set permissions to the minimum required.
Default the org to read-only token permissions.
commonMistakes: Leaving write-all permissions enabled.
Using a PAT where GITHUB_TOKEN suffices.
interviewTips: Explain why GITHUB_TOKEN cannot trigger other workflows by default.

title: OIDC to Cloud Providers
question: How does OpenID Connect (OIDC) let Actions deploy to the cloud without long-lived secrets?
answer: With OIDC, GitHub issues a short-lived signed token that a cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) trusts via a configured identity/role. The workflow exchanges it for temporary cloud credentials, eliminating stored static keys.
explanation: You configure a trust policy in the cloud that restricts which repo, branch, and environment can assume the role. This is far more secure than storing long-lived access keys as secrets.
codeExample:
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/gh-deploy
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - run: aws s3 sync ./dist s3://my-bucket
bestPractices: Scope the cloud trust policy to specific repo/branch/environment.
Grant id-token: write only where needed.
commonMistakes: Over-permissive trust conditions on the sub claim.
Falling back to long-lived keys unnecessarily.
interviewTips: Explain the sub claim and how to restrict it.

title: Self-Hosted Runners
question: When and how do you use self-hosted runners?
answer: Self-hosted runners are machines you own that execute jobs, useful for special hardware, private network access, or custom software. Register them to a repo, org, or enterprise and target them via runs-on with labels.
explanation: They give control and access to internal resources but require maintenance and hardening. Avoid using them on public repos where forked PRs could run untrusted code.
codeExample:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, gpu]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./train.sh
bestPractices: Use ephemeral runners for isolation.
Never run untrusted fork PRs on self-hosted runners.
commonMistakes: Leaving persistent runners with residual state.
Exposing internal networks to public repo workflows.
interviewTips: Discuss runner groups and autoscaling.

title: PR Reviews & Required Checks
question: How do required reviews and status checks enforce quality on pull requests?
answer: Branch protection or rulesets can require a number of approving reviews, dismiss stale approvals on new commits, and require specific status checks (CI, linting, coverage) to pass before merge. This blocks merging until quality gates are green.
explanation: Required status checks reference job/check names from your workflows. Combined with CODEOWNERS and required reviews, they ensure changes are both approved and validated.
codeExample:
# workflow whose 'test' check becomes required
name: ci
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm test
bestPractices: Require CI checks and at least one approval.
Enable dismiss stale reviews on new pushes.
commonMistakes: Marking a check required before it ever runs.
Allowing admins to bypass silently.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between required reviews and required checks.

title: GitHub CLI (gh) & Automation
question: How do you use the GitHub CLI (gh) to automate common tasks?
answer: The gh CLI lets you manage PRs, issues, releases, and workflows from the terminal or inside Actions. It authenticates via gh auth login or GH_TOKEN and is scriptable for automation.
explanation: gh is handy in local workflows and CI for creating PRs, commenting, triggering workflows, and querying the API with gh api. It reduces context switching to the web UI.
codeExample:
gh auth status
gh pr create --base main --head feature --title "Add cache" --body "Speeds up CI"
gh pr checks
gh release create v1.2.0 --generate-notes
gh workflow run ci.yml --ref main
bestPractices: Use GH_TOKEN in CI instead of interactive login.
Script repetitive triage with gh api and jq.
commonMistakes: Hardcoding tokens in scripts.
Ignoring gh exit codes in automation.
interviewTips: Show how gh replaces manual UI steps in CI.

title: Releases, Tags & Semantic Release
question: How do you automate releases and versioning with tags and semantic release?
answer: Tags mark specific commits; GitHub Releases attach notes and assets to a tag. Tools like semantic-release parse Conventional Commits to determine the next semver version, generate a changelog, tag, and publish automatically in CI.
explanation: Conventional Commits (feat, fix, BREAKING CHANGE) drive automated version bumps, removing manual versioning and ensuring consistent changelogs. Dependabot can keep dependencies current alongside this.
codeExample:
name: release
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
permissions:
  contents: write
jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npx semantic-release
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
bestPractices: Adopt Conventional Commits to drive automation.
Grant contents: write only to the release job.
commonMistakes: Manually editing versions and forgetting the changelog.
Tagging without release notes.
interviewTips: Explain how commit types map to semver bumps.
